explanation:
The requirement states that when the system enters fail-safe mode, the controller must force the input (inspiratory) valve to the closed state and the output (expiratory) valve to the open state. In terms of atomic propositions, 'failSafeMode' indicates entering fail-safe mode, 'inValveClose' indicates the input valve is closed, and 'outValveOpen' indicates the output valve is open. The property should state that globally, whenever failSafeMode is true, it must be the case that both inValveClose and outValveOpen are true. Therefore, the most appropriate LTL template is 
G (bool_exp2 -> bool_exp3),
with bool_exp2 = failSafeMode,
bool_exp3 = (inValveClose & outValveOpen).
This ensures that at all times, if the system is in fail-safe mode, the controller has indeed forced the input valve closed and output valve open.

The natural language states that in fail-safe mode, the controller forces the input (inspiratory) valve closed and the output (expiratory) valve open. The atomic proposition 'failSafeMode' indicates when fail-safe mode is active, while 'inValveClose' and 'outValveOpen' represent the status of the relevant valves. The property should enforce that whenever failSafeMode is true, 'inValveClose' and 'outValveOpen' must also be true. This is a persistent requirement, so the most direct and robust translation is: 'globally, whenever failSafeMode is true, inValveClose and outValveOpen must both be true.'

The natural language specifies that when the controller is in fail-safe mode, it should force the input valve closed and the output valve open. This can be captured by stating that globally, if 'failSafeMode' is true, then both 'inValveClose' and 'outValveOpen' must be true at any time. Template 10 matches this scenario: G (bool_exp2 -> bool_exp3), where bool_exp2 is 'failSafeMode' and bool_exp3 is 'inValveClose & outValveOpen'. This expresses at all times, whenever fail-safe mode is entered, the valves are set to their safe positions.

The property describes the desired Fail-safe behavior: when the controller is in failSafeMode, it forces the input and output valves to their de-energized states. According to the provided atomic propositions, failSafeMode identifies the system being in fail-safe mode. The required output behavior is that the inspiratory (input) valve is closed and the expiratory (output) valve is open. Thus, whenever failSafeMode is active, it must always be the case that inValveClose and outValveOpen are also true. This is best captured by the template G (failSafeMode -> (inValveClose & outValveOpen)) which asserts that at every point in the system execution, if failSafeMode holds, then both de-energized states also hold, which matches the natural language specification.

The input states that entering fail-safe mode causes the controller to force the input valve to the closed state and output valve to the open state (as safety positions). Thus, whenever 'failSafeMode' is active, it must always be the case that 'inValveClose' and 'outValveOpen' are true. This is best captured as an invariant: G(failSafeMode -> (inValveClose & outValveOpen)), i.e., globally, whenever failSafeMode holds, both inValveClose and outValveOpen must hold. This matches the input description about forcing the valves to these positions in fail-safe mode.

The property requires that, whenever the system is in failSafeMode (which represents the controller detecting a severe condition and entering the fail-safe mode), the input valve is closed and the output valve is open. This is derived from the description of failSafeMode ('in valve closed and out valve open') and the requirement that the controller forces these states in fail-safe condition. The template G (bool_exp1 -> bool_exp3) is used, where bool_exp1 is 'failSafeMode' (trigger for fail-safe mode), and bool_exp3 is '(inValveClose & outValveOpen)' (required valve states in fail-safe). Thus, the LTL expresses that globally (always), if the system is in fail-safe mode, both the in valve is closed and the out valve is open.

The requirement indicates that when the controller enters fail-safe mode (failSafeMode), it should force the input valve (inValveClose: inspiratory valve closed) and the output valve (outValveOpen: expiratory valve open) into their respective de-energized states and maintain them. This means that, globally, whenever failSafeMode is active, both these conditions on the valves must always hold. The most concise LTL template to reflect this is a globally constrained implication: whenever failSafeMode holds, inValveClose AND outValveOpen must also hold. This is captured by template 10: G (bool_exp2 -> bool_exp3), where bool_exp2 = failSafeMode, bool_exp3 = (inValveClose & outValveOpen), and N_DURATION is not required for this template.

The natural language describes a fail-safe mode where the controller forces the in-valve to close and the out-valve to open. This is triggered whenever `failSafeMode` is true, and the correct valve states (`inValveClose` and `outValveOpen`) should be enforced globally during this mode. The chosen template is 'Globally, if failSafeMode, then globally inValveClose & outValveOpen', which is best represented by 'G (failSafeMode -> (G (inValveClose & outValveOpen)))' (template 17 with a conjunction in bool_exp3). This ensures that as soon as the fail-safe is active, these valve positions hold at all future points, strictly enforcing the fail-safe safety requirement described.

The property states that whenever the controller enters fail-safe mode (failSafeMode is true), it should guarantee that the inspiratory valve is closed (inValveClose) and the output (expiratory) valve is open (outValveOpen). This matches the requirement that in fail-safe, the controller forces the valves into their safe, de-energized positions. The 'G' operator ensures this requirement is enforced globally (at all times).

The fail-safe condition described in the input natural language requires that when the system is in failSafeMode, the controller puts the inspiratory valve (input) in the closed state (inValveClose) and the expiratory valve (output) in the open state (outValveOpen). We want to ensure that, whenever failSafeMode is true, both inValveClose and outValveOpen are true, i.e., this holds globally. This is captured using the template ID 40: G((bool_exp1 & bool_exp2) -> bool_exp3), by letting bool_exp1 = failSafeMode, bool_exp2 = True (which is redundant, so only failSafeMode), and bool_exp3 = (inValveClose & outValveOpen). This asserts that, globally, whenever failSafeMode is active, the controller must force input and output valves into their fail-safe positions.
